analogy: Think of "Cell Structure and Functions" through the chapter's own opening images: FORCE AND PRESSURE FORCE AND PRESSURE In Class VII, you have learnt how objects move. Do you recall how we can decide whether an object is moving faster than the other? What does the distance moved by an object in unit time indicate? You also... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: FORCE AND PRESSURE FORCE AND PRESSURE, 8.1 Force – A Push or a Pull, SCIENCE 94, FORCE AND PRESSURE 95. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Cell Structure and Functions is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that fORCE AND PRESSURE FORCE AND PRESSURE In Class VII, you have learnt how objects move.

The text explains that do you recall how we can decide whether an object is moving faster than the other?

The text explains that what does the distance moved by an object in unit time indicate?

The text explains that you also know that a moving object like a ball rolling on the ground slows down.

The text explains that sometimes it may change its direction of motion.

The text explains that it is also possible that the ball may slow down and also change its direction.

The text explains that have you ever wondered what makes an object slow down or go faster, or change its direction of motion?

The text explains that let us recall some of our everyday experiences.

The text explains that what do you do to make a moving ball move faster?

The text explains that a hockey player changes the direction of the moving ball with a flick of the stick.

The text explains that how do fielders stop a ball hit by a batsman?

The text explains that in all these situations the ball is either made to move faster or slower or its direction of motion is changed.

The text explains that we often say that a force has been applied on a ball when it is kicked, pushed, thrown or flicked.

The text explains that what can it do to bodies on which it is applied?

The text explains that we shall seek answers to such questions in this chapter.

The text explains that 8.1 Force – A Push or a Pull A c t i o n s l i k e p i c k i n g , o p e n i n g , shutting, kicking, hitting, lifting, flicking, pushing, pulling are often used to describe certain tasks.

The text explains that 8.3 (a) : Who is pushing whom? direction of the applied force.

The text explains that sCIENCE 96 Does it mean that the net force on an object is zero if the two forces acting on it in opposite directions are equal? rope in their direction.

The text explains that forces applied on an object in the same direction add to one another.

The text explains that now recall what happened when you and your friend pushed the heavy box in the same direction in Activity 8.2.

The text explains that if the two forces act in the opposite directions on an object, the net force acting on it is the difference between the two forces.

The text explains that what did you observe in Activity 8.2 when both of you were pushing the heavy box from opposite directions?

The text explains that recall that in the tug-of-war when two teams pull equally hard, the rope does not move in any direction.

The text explains that we have also to specify the direction in which a force acts.

The text explains that also, if the direction or the magnitude of the applied force changes, its effect also changes.

The text explains that now ask one of your friends to help you in pushing it in the same direction [Fig.

The text explains that if it does, note the direction in which it moves.

The text explains that 8.4 : Two friends pushing a heavy load (a) in the same direction, (b) in opposite direction (a) (b) 8.3 Exploring Forces Let us try to learn more about forces.

The text explains that in this game two teams pull at a rope in opposite directions (Fig.

The text explains that fORCE AND PRESSURE 97 In general, more than one force may be acting on an object.

The text explains that the force applied by him can stop or deflect the ball, saving a goal being scored.

The text explains that if the force applied on the object is in the direction of its motion, the speed of the object increases.

The text explains that if the force is applied in the direction opposite to the direction of motion, then it results in a decrease in the speed of the object.

The text explains that is there any change in the direction of motion of the ball in these cases?

The text explains that in all these examples the speed and the direction of the moving ball change due to the application of a force.

The text explains that a change in either the speed of an object, or its direction of motion, or both, is described as a change in its state of motion.

The text explains that 8.8 : (a) A ball set in motion by pushing it along a level surface and (b) the direction of motion of the ball after it strikes the ruler placed in its path (a) State of Motion The state of motion of an object is described by its speed and the direction of motion.

The text explains that does the ball continue to move in the same direction after it strikes the ruler?

The text explains that in each case note your observations about the direction of motion of the ball after it strikes the ruler.

The text explains that fORCE AND PRESSURE 99 8.5 Force can Change the Shape of an Object Description of Situation How to Apply Force Diagram Action of Force Change in State of Motion Change in Shape Yes No Yes No Pressing it down with your hands.

The text explains that having performed all the above activities, you would have realised that a force l may make an object move from rest. l may change the speed of an object if it is moving. l may change the direction of motion of an object. l may bring about a change in the shape of an object. l may cause some or all of these effects.

The text explains that thus, an object cannot move by itself, it cannot change speed by itself, it cannot change direction by itself and its shape cannot change by itself.

The text explains that in Class VII you have learnt that in the process of digestion the food gets pushed through the alimentary canal.

The text explains that fORCE AND PRESSURE 101 Since muscular force can be applied only when it is in contact with an object, it is also called a contact force.

The text explains that can you guess the direction in which the force must be acting in each case?

The text explains that cover the mouth of the tumbler with a thick card similar to that of a postcard.

The text explains that hold the tumbler with one hand while keeping the card pressed to its mouth with your other hand.

The text explains that turn the tumbler upside down while keeping the card pressed to its mouth.

The text explains that does the card get detached allowing the water to spill?

The text explains that with a little practice you will find that the card continues to hold water in the tumbler even after it is not supported by your hand.

The text explains that also try this activity by using a piece of cloth to hold the tumbler in an upside down position (Fig.

The text explains that take 4-5 plastic bottles of different shapes and sizes.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Science, Chapter 8: Cell Structure and Functions

Do not add outside examples to Cell Structure and Functions; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
